claim: The Currier A/B split reflects two distinct scribal hands encoding the same language using different glyph-frequency profiles, such that the top-10 word overlap between A and B sections is less than 40%, and the word-initial glyph distributions of A and B differ by Jensen-Shannon divergence > 0.15
evidence_for: Currier A contains 11,022 words vs B's 23,766 â€” a substantial split suggesting systematic difference
Current belief [0.456] supports a genuine linguistic or dialectal difference
High hapax ratio (70.1%) could reflect two vocabularies being conflated
evidence_against: If the same cipher is used with different keys, vocabulary overlap could still be low without reflecting language difference
A/B distinction may be purely codicological (different sections, not different scribes)
test: Compute Jensen-Shannon divergence between word-initial and word-final glyph frequency distributions of Currier A folios vs Currier B folios; also compute vocabulary overlap (shared word types / union of word types)
test_metric: JS divergence of glyph distributions and Jaccard similarity of vocabularies
test_threshold: JS divergence > 0.15 and Jaccard < 0.40 would support distinct encoding keys or languages; JS < 0.05 and Jaccard > 0.60 would support a single cipher

tests_run.test: compute jensen-shannon divergence between word-initial and word-final glyph frequency distributions
tests_run.details: Language A: entropy=3.8321, 3513 unique/11022 total words. Language B: entropy=3.8611, 5089 unique/23766 total words. Entropy difference: 0.0290. Vocabulary density difference: 0.1046.

reasoning: Distinguishing whether A/B is a cipher-key difference or a language difference is a high-leverage split: if JS is large but vocabulary overlap is moderate, a polyalphabetic key change is implicated; if both diverge strongly, distinct languages are implicated
